name: Platform-neutral procurement governance for Danish digital identity
consequences: Immediate: Procurement-language updates are published and governance bodies are established to oversee compliance. → Systemic: Supplier concentration declines by 25% (Herfindahl-index) and annual platform-ecosystem costs fall by 12% due to diversified sourcing. → Strategic: Denmark achieves stronger digital sovereignty and continuity, but may encounter higher initial procurement overhead and longer onboarding cycles for new vendors.
options: Codify platform-neutral requirements into AltID procurement documents and standard contractual clauses to constrain vendor lock-in.
Establish an independent procurement oversight body to audit supplier concentration and enforce fallback-ready standards across identity procurements.

Create a European open-platform consortium that operates a shared neutral trust-anchor service using decentralized identities (DIDs) and verifiable credentials, funded by a member cooperative with public subsidies.
review: Controls platform-dependence vs governance agility. Weakness: The options fail to consider transitional costs and the timeline risk for Phase 1 deliverables.

name: Platform-neutral fallback authentication demonstration
consequences: Immediate: Non-production demonstrators are designed and lab-tested against a simulated MitID environment. → Systemic: Interoperability readiness improves by 0.25 on a 1.0 scale; vendor-lock-in risk decreases by 15%. → Strategic: The project gains credibility for procurement-language changes and EU alignment, but may face schedule risk and higher R&D costs.
options: Demonstrate a browser-based fallback path using WebAuthn/FIDO2 on GrapheneOS to decouple from OS signals.
Develop a mobile-oriented demonstrator with hardware-token-based fallback and offline verification using standardized attestation.
Establish an open-source, Europe-wide fallback service integrating a DID-based wallet with portable hardware tokens and a regional testbed.
review: Controls credibility of fallback demonstrations vs budget constraints. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term interoperability governance and ongoing funding beyond demonstration.

name: EU interoperability leverage and standards alignment
consequences: Immediate: Denmark engages EU working groups and returns feedback. → Systemic: The number of EU-approved standards references increases by 2 within two years; participation costs rise by about 0.8 million DKK/year. → Strategic: Denmark strengthens cross-border interoperability, but risk of diluting national specifics and lengthening negotiation cycles.
options: Submit detailed policy positions to EU Digital Identity Wallet working groups to influence requirements.
Contribute to AltID interoperability test suites and advocate for platform-neutral APIs within NSIS.
Forge a cross-border European platform-neutral identity alliance with shared governance and a joint funding pool to sustain independent trust anchors.
review: Controls EU alignment vs national autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to consider potential friction from EU-wide regulatory harmonization timelines.

name: Resilience coalition and procurement risk management
consequences: Immediate: Coalition formed; risk-mapping delivered. → Systemic: Vendor-risk exposure declines by 20%; redundancy-related procurement cost increases by ~3%. → Strategic: Improves continuity and resilience, but increases near-term budget pressure and requires ongoing coalition management.
options: Form a formal coalition with civil society and academia to publish risk exposure metrics and resilience scenarios.
Pilot guaranteed-availability procurement clauses with backup vendors and defined SLAs in future AltID procurements.
Create a sovereign buy-in program with government-backed guarantees to fund fallback infrastructure and cross-provider incident-response readiness.
review: Controls resilience of supply chain vs procurement flexibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider dynamic geopolitical risk thresholds and re-pricing of fallback services.

name: Platform-agnostic authentication abstraction layer
consequences: Immediate: API abstraction layer specification completed and lab-tested. → Systemic: Device support expands by 3x and integration cost rises by ~12% due to added indirection; time-to-provision increases. → Strategic: Enables true platform neutrality and future-proofing but raises initial complexity and requires cross-domain governance for API design.
options: Develop a standards-based API abstraction layer decoupling applications from platform integrity signals.
Prototype a cross-platform trust-signal broker translating platform attestations to a common Verifiable Credential format.
Launch an open-source, modular identity stack with edge-based attestations and a marketplace of cross-provider trust signals.
review: Controls performance vs complexity. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory constraints around data localization and cross-border attestation storage.

name: Resilience framing and public engagement strategy
consequences: Immediate: Risk framing plan rolled out; stakeholder meetings initiated. → Systemic: Public sentiment toward sovereignty improves by 15% and policy-readiness index increases by 0.4; risk of politicization if messaging is overly aggressive. → Strategic: Increases political bandwidth for platform-neutral policy; but potential backlash from platform incumbents.
options: Develop a risk-communication plan highlighting sovereignty, continuity risks, and cost implications of platform lock-in.
Publish scenario analyses and contingency planning materials to inform Parliament and EU committees.
Launch a documentary-style, cross-border advocacy campaign with academic partners and industry stakeholders to shift public opinion and policy attention.
review: Controls public sentiment vs policy risk. Weakness: The options fail to quantify the risk of misinformation or deliberate mischaracterization by platform incumbents.

name: AltID platform-neutral procurement standards for Danish digital credentials
consequences: Immediate: Adoption of the clause templates begins in pilot procurements. → Systemic: 22% reduction in reliance on platform integrity signals, measured by the share of authentication events validated by platform-neutral attestations; Trade-off: Accelerates platform-neutral adoption but increases governance overhead. → Strategic: Over time, this enables stronger resilience and EU interoperability momentum, while requiring sustained governance and budgetary support.
options: Adopt a platform-neutral procurement clause bundle that requires fallback authentication pathways in all new AltID-related contracts.
Pilot platform-neutral fallback requirements within a controlled procurement program to test interfaces and governance against existing MitID dependencies.
Establish a decentralized credential marketplace using decentralized identifiers (DID) and verifiable credentials to displace platform-signaled trust anchors in AltID contracts.
review: Controls Platform-neutrality vs. platform-dependency. Weakness: The options fail to consider the depth of regulatory alignment required for fallback pathways and the potential pushback from incumbent operators.

name: Fallback authentication ecosystem integration across Denmark's public services
consequences: Immediate: Browser-based and hardware-token fallback pathways are implemented in pilot government portals. → Systemic: 12% reduction in downtime during platform outages, measured by average outage duration; Trade-off: additional hardware-token maintenance costs and user onboarding friction. → Strategic: Strengthens continuity resilience, but raises OPEX and user support burden.
options: Integrate a browser-based fallback authenticator into a pilot set of government portals under NSIS-aligned testbeds.
Develop a hardware-token backed fallback path with minimal user friction and standard OpenID Connect flows.
Establish a universal fallback across public services using open hardware-tokens with cross-portal attestations to reduce OS duopoly reliance.
review: Controls Continuity vs. Convenience. Weakness: The options fail to consider the long-term user education and recovery-process requirements for hardware tokens.

name: EU interoperability acceleration through wallet-level standards alignment
consequences: Immediate: EU wallet standards working group participation begins and API prototypes are drafted. → Systemic: 25% of cross-border test flows succeed in interoperability pilots; Trade-off: standardization speed vs domestic customization. → Strategic: Positions Denmark within EU-level interoperability, but may constrain domestic flexibility.
options: Lobby for explicit inclusion of Danish AltID in EU wallet standards with minimal changes to current architecture.
Contribute to EU working groups to draft interoperable APIs and NSIS extensions used by AltID.
Lead a cross-border, platform-neutral wallet federation using European Digital Identity Wallet standards with shared attestation and revocation services.
review: Controls Sovereign interoperability vs EU-level standardization. Weakness: The options fail to consider potential delays in EU timetable and the risk of domestic rigidity.

name: Resilience governance and procurement incentives coalition
consequences: Immediate: Resilience coalition sign-off and procurement language updates go live. → Systemic: 30% higher probability of platform-neutral contract adoption by phase two bids; Trade-off: administrative overhead vs resilience. → Strategic: Builds durable policy momentum, but invites pushback from incumbents and budgetary scrutiny.
options: Formalize a resilience coalition with civil society and vendors to publish a shared procurement risk register and staged funding.
Introduce procurement incentives such as preferred supplier status for platform-neutral compliance and early risk-sharing agreements.
Create a public-private resilience fund and open-call for platform-neutral fallback bi-lateral contracts with performance-based milestones.
review: Controls Procurement leverage vs. incumbency resistance. Weakness: The options fail to consider budget volatility and phase-gate governance beyond a single fiscal year.

name: Open-source verifier network and cross-signer attestation for trust anchors
consequences: Immediate: Open-source verifier libraries are contributed and initial cross-signer tests run. → Systemic: 28% increase in trust-anchor diversity under test conditions; Trade-off: governance complexity and maintenance costs. → Strategic: Improves resilience and interoperability, but requires sustained funding and governance.
options: Contribute to open-source verification libraries and define cross-signer attestation standards for vendor-neutral trust anchors.
Pilot a shared attestation service with multiple signers across public, academic, and private sectors to reduce single-entity risk.
Deploy a global, permissioned ledger of attestations tied to a DID ecosystem operated by a consortium, enabling real-time revocation and cross-jurisdiction trust.
review: Controls Attestation trust vs. governance burden. Weakness: The options fail to address the need for legal alignment of cross-border attestations and revocation policies.

name: Decentralized identity wallet pilot with user-controlled keys
consequences: Immediate: Decentralized wallet pilot with opt-in key control is launched with a defined recovery process. → Systemic: 9% reduction in platform-dependent authentication flows across core services (pilot metric); Trade-off: user friction and key recovery challenges. → Strategic: Shifts trust from platform anchors to user-controlled identity, but requires robust education, privacy governance, and policy updates.
options: Pilot a user-controlled identity wallet on a limited cohort with opt-in recovery mechanisms and clear privacy controls.
Expand to broader user base with risk-based authentication and support for account recovery and key backup.
Deploy a fully decentralized identity wallet with self-sovereign identity principles and cross-domain portability across EU services.
review: Controls User autonomy vs. usability. Weakness: The options fail to fully address regulatory scrutiny around self-sovereign identity and data portability.

name: Platform-neutral fallback authentication governance
consequences: Immediate: Establishes governance anchors for platform-neutral fallback pathways and clarifies responsibilities across agencies → Systemic: Non-platform-authentication traffic share rises from 5% to 25% within two years, reducing vendor-concentration risk by 20 percentage points; procurement complexity increases by 1.5 percentage points of the projected budget → Strategic: Strengthens Denmark’s digital sovereignty and continuity of service, but increases short-term compliance overhead and slows scale-up due to stricter vendor criteria. Trade-off: resilience gains vs. procurement overhead.
options: Codify platform-neutral fallback requirements into AltID procurement language, mandate NSIS-aligned controls, and require multi-vendor testing and reporting.
Pilot a cross-ecosystem fallback path using open standards (FIDO2/WebAuthn) on diverse devices in two ministries to validate interoperability.
Launch a decentralized fallback as a service using a DID-based sovereign wallet and a public-private governance fund to finance continuity testing.
review: Controls resilience of access vs. efficiency of procurement processes. Weakness: The options fail to consider data sovereignty and cross-border privacy constraints that may complicate NSIS alignment and eIDAS2 compliance.

name: EU wallet interoperability standards alignment through procurement leverage
consequences: Immediate: Signals commitment to EU wallet standards and aligns Danish procurement with cross-border requirements → Systemic: Cross-border interoperability success rate improves from 52% to 75% within 18 months, reducing integration cycle time by 22%; cost delta adds ~2.5% of the program budget due to extended testing and compliance work → Strategic: Elevates Denmark as a compliant, forward-looking member in EU wallet initiatives, but increases ongoing regulatory compliance overhead and monitoring.
options: Lock procurement to EU wallet standards and require explicit eIDAS2 alignment across AltID components.
Create a joint standards workstream with Danish agencies and EU bodies to pilot cross-border verification in two public services.
Design a wallet-agnostic platform architecture with a modular verification interface, financed through an EU-wide public-private consortium.
review: Controls cross-border interoperability vs internal policy feasibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider divergent EU member-state timelines and procurement restrictions that could impede harmonization.

name: Supplier-diversification through shared-service fallback marketplace
consequences: Immediate: Establishes a marketplace-style procurement approach to reduce vendor lock-in → Systemic: Vendor count grows from 2 to 5 suppliers; fallback market share reaches 25% of authentication capacity; cost overhead rises by approximately 2% of the program budget → Strategic: Improves resilience and competition but adds governance overhead and ongoing marketplace management requirements.
options: Diversify procurement criteria to encourage multiple credible vendors with independent security audits and open-source verifiability.
Institute phased procurement with independent security reviews to prevent single-vendor dominance and to mandate open interface standards.
Create a fallback marketplace as a service (FaaS) using a public-private cooperative and open-source verifiers, financed by a governance fund.
review: Controls supplier diversity vs marketplace governance efficiency. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory constraints on public procurement and compatibility with MitID ecosystems.

name: Non-production demonstrator alignment with Open Standards
consequences: Immediate: Establishes two credible non-production demonstrators to test WebAuthn/OpenID Connect on privacy-respecting devices and browser-token fallbacks → Systemic: Demonstrator success rate in simulated environments rises from 60% to 85% with 3–6 months of iteration; hardware costs drift by +3% but risk exposure declines by 18% → Strategic: Builds credible technical evidence for platform-neutrality, while not committing to production deployment and potentially stretching phase-two timelines.
options: Establish a non-production demonstrator framework using open standards on privacy-preserving devices to evaluate fallback viability.
Scale to browser-based and hardware-token fallback demos across two public-sector pilots to validate cross-platform viability.
Launch a radical demonstrator employing a decentralized identity network with sovereign wallets and verifiable attestations, funded via a governance-token model.
review: Controls technical feasibility vs policy alignment. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term licensing, maintenance costs, and governance of open-source components.

name: Policy language and EU wallet alignment for AltID resilience
consequences: Immediate: Drafts procurement and policy language to remove exclusive platform dependencies in AltID → Systemic: EU wallet alignment readiness index improves from 45% to 68%, cross-border acceptance reduces integration effort by 15%, and ongoing compliance costs rise modestly by 1–1.5% → Strategic: Enhances Denmark’s influence in EU wallet initiatives and reduces long-term costs of compliance, but requires sustained budgetary commitment and political support.
options: Draft procurement language binding platform neutrality with a defined 2029 deadline and measurable milestones.
Secure formal EU-aligned commitments to interoperability in AltID-related procurement documents across agencies.
Propose a transnational procurement framework using a shared-risk ledger and performance-based funding to support a fallback-enabled authentication network across EU member states.
review: Controls sovereignty vs EU harmonization. Weakness: The options fail to account for domestic political risk and budget volatility that could derail long-term commitments.
